title: Baseline Configuration Deviation — Key OS and Application Settings

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

app: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742)
spl:
index=config sourcetype=config:snapshot earliest=-24h
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| lookup bes_config_baseline.csv host, setting_name OUTPUT expected_value
| where expected_value!=current_value
| table host, setting_name, expected_value, current_value, bes_system
description: We compare configuration snapshots from servers and appliances to approved baselines so unauthorized drift on BES Cyber Assets is visible.
value: We compare configuration snapshots from servers and appliances to approved baselines so unauthorized drift on BES Cyber Assets is visible.
grandmaExplanation: We compare configuration snapshots from servers and appliances to approved baselines so unauthorized drift on BES Cyber Assets is visible — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Collect snapshots via scripted input or Chef/Ansible exports. (2) Version baselines with change tickets. (3) Alert on critical settings only first. (4) Feed drift into change review board.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=config` `sourcetype=config:snapshot`, `bes_config_baseline.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Collect snapshots via scripted input or Chef/Ansible exports. (2) Version baselines with change tickets. (3) Alert on critical settings only first. (4) Feed drift into change review board.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=config sourcetype=config:snapshot earliest=-24h
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| lookup bes_config_baseline.csv host, setting_name OUTPUT expected_value
| where expected_value!=current_value
| table host, setting_name, expected_value, current_value, bes_system
```

Understanding this SPL

**Baseline Configuration Deviation — Key OS and Application Settings (CIP-010-4 R1 Part 1.1)** — We compare configuration snapshots from servers and appliances to approved baselines so unauthorized drift on BES Cyber Assets is visible.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=config` `sourcetype=config:snapshot`, `bes_config_baseline.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: config; **sourcetype**: config:snapshot. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=config, sourcetype=config:snapshot, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(bes_system)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where expected_value!=current_value` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Baseline Configuration Deviation — Key OS and Application Settings (CIP-010-4 R1 Part 1.1)**): table host, setting_name, expected_value, current_value, bes_system


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (drift rows), Bar chart (by setting), Single value (host count)

Scripted input (generic example)
This use case relies on a scripted input. In the app's local/inputs.conf add a stanza such as:

```ini
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/YourApp/bin/collect.sh]
interval = 300
sourcetype = your_sourcetype
index = main
disabled = 0
```

The script should print one event per line (e.g. key=value). Example minimal script (bash):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Output metrics or events, one per line
echo "metric=value timestamp=$(date +%s)"
```

For full details (paths, scheduling, permissions), see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
